result:
I’ve been thinking lately about the way light behaves at the edges of things—how it frays like old cloth, how it refuses to stay inside the lines we draw. Outside my window, the last streetlamp is switching itself off just as the sky turns the exact color of a robin’s egg left in water too long. Neither moment is proud of the other; they simply hand the world back and forth without applause.

Inside, the kettle clicks. That small sound is the house’s heartbeat, a reminder that we build shelters around ourselves and then wonder why we feel enclosed. I used to believe the cure was more space—bigger rooms, taller ceilings—but now I suspect the remedy is smaller: leave the front door cracked so the smell of rain can wander in, keep one unwashed coffee cup on the counter so yesterday’s scent of grounds lingers like an unpaid bill. Memory needs loose pockets; it leaks out if the seams are too tight.

Yesterday I found a sealed envelope addressed to someone I was three addresses ago. The handwriting looked like mine, only braver. I didn’t open it. Instead I taped it to the refrigerator, a ransom note from a former self who still thinks he can negotiate. Maybe tomorrow I’ll slide it under the door of a stranger and let us both guess at the terms.

People keep asking what I’m working on, as if the answer should arrive pre-packaged, vacuum-sealed. I want to tell them I’m cultivating an archive of almost: the wingbeat of a moth that didn’t quite land, the breath a violinist takes before the first down-bow, the hush between the phone’s final ring and the caller deciding to speak. That pause is a country I’m trying to map; its borders dissolve as soon as my pen nears them.

The radio claims a new exoplanet has been discovered where it rains molten glass, sideways, at seven thousand kilometers an hour. I picture the sky as a cathedral of liquid prisms, every falling shard a reminder that somewhere the laws are different, that what shatters here might arrive there already whole. I hold my coffee cup under the faucet and watch water swirl counterclockwise down the drain; physicists say that if the universe flipped in a mirror, the spiral would reverse. I test the idea with my left hand, but the water doesn’t notice. It obeys only the shape of the sink, the tilt of the earth, the stories we tell it.

Evening arrives earlier than scheduled, carrying a suitcase full of moths. They scatter when I switch on the lamp, tiny collisions of dust and intention. One lands on my wrist, trembles like a false pulse. I decide not to move until it chooses elsewhere, a small treaty with uncertainty. Eventually it lifts, leaving a powdery constellation I rub away before it can name itself.

Somewhere down the block a dog barks once, a terse punctuation mark. Somewhere else a child refuses to sleep until the moon is put back in its crib. I sit down to write, not because I have something to say, but because the silence is crowded and words are the least destructive passengers I can invite.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
